items.content_statement: The Treaty of Waitangi | te Tiriti o Waitangi is important as New Zealand's founding agreement between the British Crown and Māori rangatira (chiefs)
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about the historical significance and parties to the Treaty - factual content to be understood

items.content_statement: The Treaty of Waitangi | te Tiriti o Waitangi was first signed on 6 February 1840 at Waitangi in the Bay of Islands.
items.classification_rationale: Factual historical information - date, location, and event details that constitute propositional knowledge to be held

items.content_statement: The Treaty of Waitangi | te Tiriti o Waitangi has ongoing significance as a symbol of mutual respect, shared heritage, and commitment to working together
items.classification_rationale: Propositional claim about the ongoing significance and symbolic meaning of the Treaty - factual/conceptual content to be understood

items.content_statement: There are two documents: the Treaty of Waitangi in English and te Tiriti o Waitangi in te reo Māori.
items.classification_rationale: Factual propositional knowledge about the existence and language versions of these historical documents

items.content_statement: James Busby acted as the British Resident in New Zealand from 1833. He played a role in developing the first New Zealand flag in 1834 (United Tribes flag) and helped to facilitate the drafting of the Treaty of Waitangi in 1840.
items.classification_rationale: Step 1: This content describes factual historical information about James Busby's role and actions - propositional knowledge that learners must hold as facts.

items.content_statement: William Hobson was the first Governor of New Zealand. He proposed a treaty with Māori to establish British sovereignty.
items.classification_rationale: Historical facts about specific people, events, and their roles - propositional knowledge that learners must hold

items.content_statement: Henry Williams translated the Treaty of Waitangi into te reo Māori with his son Edward and explained it to Māori chiefs. As the leader of the Church Missionary Society in New Zealand, he contributed to the spread of Christianity among Māori and expanded colonial settlement. He also attempted to prevent hostilities at Kororāreka in 1845.
items.classification_rationale: Historical facts about Henry Williams' role in Treaty translation and colonial activities - propositional knowledge that can be assessed through knowledge test

items.content_statement: Hōne Heke was the first Māori chief to sign the Treaty of Waitangi in 1840. Later, he led Māori resistance against British colonial authority over Treaty breaches and to protect rangatiratanga. He ordered the cutting of the British flagstaff at Kororāreka four times between 1844 and 1845, sparking the Northern War.
items.classification_rationale: Historical facts and sequence of events - propositional knowledge that can be assessed through knowledge test

items.content_statement: Promotion of New Zealand landscapes to Victorian travellers. An early site of tourism.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional/factual content about historical tourism development - Type 1 factual knowledge

items.content_statement: Devastating impact through loss of life; loss of landscape. Impact on the livelihoods of Māori — guiding and tourism.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about causal impacts that learners must understand as factual content

items.content_statement: Subsequent development (1931) of Buried Village of Te Wairoa as a tourist attraction and archaeological site.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge - factual content about a specific historical development that learners must hold

items.content_statement: Newspaper articles, first-hand accounts, and photographic evidence can enrich this study
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about what types of sources can enhance historical study - factual content about research methodology

items.content_statement: Sealing and whaling from the 1790s, including port towns and trading posts, and Māori–Pākehā individuals like Barnet Burns
items.classification_rationale: Historical factual content about specific economic activities, timeframes, locations, and individuals - propositional knowledge to be held

items.content_statement: Māori provision of timber, flax, and food in exchange for muskets, iron goods, and blankets
items.classification_rationale: Historical fact about trade relationships - propositional knowledge that learners must hold

items.content_statement: Impact of new diseases (measles, influenza) on Māori population
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about historical cause-and-effect relationships that learners must understand

items.content_statement: Arrival of Christian missionaries: provision of European farming tools, healthcare, and schooling
items.classification_rationale: Historical factual content about what missionaries provided - propositional knowledge with no prerequisite sequence

items.content_statement: Establishment of a te reo Māori press, distribution of translations, and rapid growth of literacy
items.classification_rationale: Historical facts about specific events and developments - propositional knowledge that can be assessed through knowledge checks

items.content_statement: Māori adaptation of Christian ideas alongside traditional Indigenous spiritual practices
items.classification_rationale: This describes a historical concept about cultural adaptation and religious syncretism that learners must understand as propositional knowledge

items.content_statement: Petitions to King William IV and other Crown appeals by rangatira seeking protection, redress, or recognition
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about historical events and documents - factual content that learners must hold

items.content_statement: 1835 Declaration of Independence | He Whakaputanga signed by rangatira
items.classification_rationale: Historical fact about a specific event - propositional knowledge that learners must hold

items.content_statement: Early land-purchase deeds (New Zealand Company), contrasting Māori concepts of use, tūrangawaewae, and mana versus the Crown's notion of permanent sale
items.classification_rationale: This content describes analytical capability across multiple cultural and legal lenses - understanding how different worldviews (Māori versus Crown) interpret the same historical events. No single correct answer but better and worse analyses of the contrasts.

items.content_statement: Musket Wars (c.1810s–1830s): causes, major campaigns, and consequences for population movements and tribal boundaries. Hongi Hika's role.
items.classification_rationale: Historical factual content about specific events, causes, consequences, and key figures - propositional knowledge that can be assessed through knowledge checks

items.content_statement: The expression of mana through Nunuku's Law and the renunciation of violence even in the face of great external challenges (the arrival of Europeans from the 1790s and of Ngāti Tama and Ngāti Mutunga from 1835)
items.classification_rationale: This describes a specific historical concept about how mana was expressed through particular practices in response to historical events - propositional knowledge about a historical phenomenon

items.content_statement: examples of different ways that people commemorate Waitangi Day across New Zealand
items.classification_rationale: factual content about specific commemorative practices - propositional knowledge

items.content_statement: why the Treaty of Waitangi | te Tiriti o Waitangi is important to public life in New Zealand today
items.classification_rationale: conceptual understanding of the Treaty's ongoing significance - transferable conceptual idea

items.content_statement: how information can be collected about the signing of the Treaty of Waitangi | te Tiriti o Waitangi
items.classification_rationale: analytical capability for historical inquiry - occasion-triggered skill deployed when research situations arise

items.content_statement: Analyse how Māori social, economic, and cultural practices changed following natural events such as the Tarawera eruption, considering consequences on trade, settlement, population and environments
items.classification_rationale: Analytical capability deployed when examining historical change - multiple valid interpretive frameworks exist, reasoning quality determines assessment outcome

items.content_statement: Analyse how legislation and town planning evolved following natural events
items.classification_rationale: Analytical capability deployed when examining historical change - multiple valid interpretive frameworks exist, reasoning quality determines assessment outcome

items.content_statement: Use sources such as newspaper articles, first-hand accounts, and photographic evidence to reconstruct historical narratives
items.classification_rationale: Analytical capability deployed when working with historical sources - multiple valid interpretive approaches exist, reasoning quality determines assessment outcome

items.content_statement: Study historical documents and artefacts to understand how and why societies and events change over time
items.classification_rationale: Analytical capability deployed when examining historical evidence - multiple valid interpretive frameworks exist, reasoning quality determines assessment outcome

items.content_statement: Analyse how Māori social, economic, and cultural practices changed with the arrival of European settlers
items.classification_rationale: Analytical capability requiring multiple interpretive lenses to examine historical change - Type 2

items.content_statement: Analyse the consequences of European arrival on trade and settlement
items.classification_rationale: Analytical capability requiring examination of cause-effect relationships through historical reasoning - Type 2

items.content_statement: Analyse the causes of the Musket Wars
items.classification_rationale: Analytical capability requiring examination of historical causation through multiple factors - Type 2

items.content_statement: Analyse the consequences of missionaries, literacy, and cultural exchange on Māori society
items.classification_rationale: Analytical capability requiring examination of complex social and cultural change - Type 2

items.content_statement: Analyse the changes to population and environments over the period
items.classification_rationale: Analytical capability requiring examination of demographic and environmental change over time - Type 2

items.content_statement: Location and geographical features of Ancient Greek city states
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about factual content - geographic locations and features that can be learned and assessed through knowledge checks

items.content_statement: Timeline of key periods: Archaic, Classical, Hellenistic
items.classification_rationale: Factual content about historical periodization - propositional knowledge that learners must hold

items.content_statement: How society was structured in Ancient Greece, including role of women and slaves and daily life
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about historical facts and social structures that can be learned and assessed through knowledge checks

items.content_statement: What the role of government was, with a focus on democracy and empire
items.classification_rationale: This describes analytical capability across multiple lenses - examining government roles through different frameworks (democracy vs empire). Multiple valid interpretive approaches exist for understanding governmental roles in Ancient Greece, with reasoning quality determining assessment outcome.

items.content_statement: How Sparta and Corinth were different from Athens (as city states)
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about historical differences between city-states - factual/conceptual content to be understood

items.content_statement: The importance of religion: gods, temples, and festivals (Olympics, Dionysia).
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about the role and significance of religion in Ancient Greece - a conceptual understanding that can be assessed through explanation of how religious elements functioned in Greek society

items.content_statement: Development of philosophy and importance of philosophers including: Socrates, Plato, and Aristotle
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about historical development and significance of specific philosophers - factual content that can be assessed through knowledge test

items.content_statement: The importance of Art, architecture, literature, and drama (tragedy and comedy)
items.classification_rationale: Propositional claim about the significance/value of cultural elements in Ancient Greece - factual/conceptual content to be understood

items.content_statement: Significance of key battles in the Persian Wars (Marathon, Thermopylae, Salamis)
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about historical significance - factual/conceptual content with clear criteria for understanding

items.content_statement: The Peloponnesian War: causes and effect on Greek unity and identity
items.classification_rationale: This content describes analytical capability across multiple lenses - understanding historical causation and effects requires examining political, social, and cultural factors through different interpretive frameworks. Multiple valid analyses possible with varying sophistication.

items.content_statement: Contributions to government, philosophy, science, architecture made by Ancient Greece
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about historical contributions - factual content that can be learned and assessed through knowledge checks

items.content_statement: Flood control and millet/rice paddy fields — building ditches and fields held back floods
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about specific agricultural techniques and their flood control function

items.content_statement: Creation of reliable agricultural surpluses for towns
items.classification_rationale: Conceptual understanding of the causal relationship between agricultural techniques and urban development

items.content_statement: Hereditary kings directed religious rituals although not considered divine
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about the role and status of hereditary kings in ancient China - factual content to be held

items.content_statement: Kings made law, raised taxes, led armies
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about the specific functions and powers of kings - factual content to be held

items.content_statement: Leaders in regions helped the king govern
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about the governmental structure and delegation of authority - factual content to be held

items.content_statement: Social hierarchy: king → priests → warriors → artisans → farmers → slaves.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about a specific historical social structure that learners must hold as factual content

items.content_statement: Timber houses on raised earth platforms — homes built up high stayed dry in floods and kept people safe
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about ancient Chinese housing design and its functional purposes - factual content to be understood

items.content_statement: Clan-based villages
items.classification_rationale: Factual knowledge about ancient Chinese social organization - propositional content about village structure

items.content_statement: Cowrie shells were used as currency in ancient China
items.classification_rationale: Propositional/factual content about historical practices - Type 1 factual knowledge

items.content_statement: Bronze, jade, and silk were traded across long distances on the silk road
items.classification_rationale: Propositional/factual content about historical trade practices - Type 1 factual knowledge

items.content_statement: Chariots with spoked wheels and yokes
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about historical technological developments - factual content to be known

items.content_statement: Silk weaving and hemp textiles
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about historical technological developments - factual content to be known

items.content_statement: Education in the Elizabethan and Victorian ages was mostly for boys of higher status, with many children working from a young age, and around 30% of boys and 10% of girls learning to read
items.classification_rationale: Factual historical information about education patterns and literacy rates - propositional knowledge to be held

items.content_statement: Monarch's power versus power of Parliament or the people
items.classification_rationale: This describes analytical capability across multiple lenses - comparing power dynamics between different political entities across historical periods. Multiple valid interpretive frameworks exist for analyzing power relationships, and reasoning quality determines assessment outcome.

items.content_statement: Elizabeth I ruled England from 1558 to 1603
items.classification_rationale: Factual propositional content with no prerequisite sequence - historical fact to be known

items.content_statement: The monarch seen as the main ruler, an 'absolute monarch', with 'government' meaning the queen and her officials.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional/factual content about historical governmental structure - definitional and factual claims that learners must hold as knowledge

items.content_statement: Parliament as a group of powerful men who helped make laws and grant taxes and did not represent ordinary people
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about the composition and function of Parliament - factual content to be understood

items.content_statement: Parliament's limited power to challenge the queen
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about the relationship between Parliament and monarchy - factual content to be understood

items.content_statement: Most people had no say in choosing its members
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about democratic representation in historical context - factual content to be understood

items.content_statement: Protestant religious rules and practices
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about specific historical religious systems - factual content to be learned

items.content_statement: How Protestant religious rules and practices were enforced across England
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about historical enforcement mechanisms - factual content to be learned

items.content_statement: The treatment of people who continued to follow Catholic beliefs
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about historical treatment of religious minorities - factual content to be learned

items.content_statement: Growth of exploration and overseas ventures and privateering, including links with the Americas and Africa
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about historical developments - factual content about what happened during these periods

items.content_statement: Invention of the printing press in 1485
items.classification_rationale: Factual historical information - propositional knowledge that must be held

items.content_statement: Growth of printing
items.classification_rationale: Factual historical information - propositional knowledge that must be held

items.content_statement: Influence of Renaissance and works of William Shakespeare
items.classification_rationale: Conceptual understanding of historical influence - transferable idea about cultural impact

items.content_statement: Features of upper, middle, and working classes and underclass of destitute in the Elizabethan and Victorian ages
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about historical social structures - factual content that must be held

items.content_statement: Role of women in the Elizabethan and Victorian ages
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about historical gender roles - factual content that must be held

items.content_statement: Steady rise in average wages across the population during Queen Victoria's reign, with more jobs and greater availability of everyday goods in shops
items.classification_rationale: Propositional/factual content about historical economic conditions - hard prerequisite sequence applies as this is foundational historical knowledge

items.content_statement: Monarch's power versus power of Parliament or the people
items.classification_rationale: This describes analytical capability across multiple lenses - comparing power structures between different historical periods requires reasoning through multiple interpretive frameworks with no single correct answer but better and worse analyses

items.content_statement: Queen Victoria was monarch of the United Kingdom from 1837 to 1901
items.classification_rationale: Factual propositional content with no prerequisite sequence - historical fact to be known

items.content_statement: Queen Victoria did not have a role in daily decisions of government. Limited political power in a constitutional monarchy, with decisions made by elected government.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional/factual content about historical political structures - hard prerequisite sequence applies as understanding constitutional monarchy concepts builds on foundational knowledge

items.content_statement: Government as prime minister and cabinet now drawn from the majority grouping of members of Parliament (MPs) in Parliament
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about how parliamentary government is structured - factual content about institutional arrangements

items.content_statement: Parliament still made up of the House of Lords and House of Commons
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about parliamentary composition - factual content about institutional structure

items.content_statement: Expanding voting rights, although working-class people remained excluded for much of the reign. No women could vote.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional/factual content about historical voting rights - hard prerequisite sequence applies

items.content_statement: Largely Christian society with emergence of scientific challenge (e.g. Darwin's theory of evolution).
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about historical characteristics - factual content about societal composition and scientific developments that learners must hold

items.content_statement: Trade and exploration and changes in buildings, technology, and inventions
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about historical developments that can be factually learned and assessed

items.content_statement: Expansion of global empire and trade networks, with links to Africa, Asia, and Oceania, including New Zealand.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional/factual content about historical developments that learners must hold as knowledge

items.content_statement: Technological innovation, such as the telegraph, photography, gas lighting, and public sanitation systems. Rapid industrialisation.
items.classification_rationale: Factual content about historical developments - propositional knowledge with no prerequisite sequence indicated

items.content_statement: Compare similarities and differences between Athens and other Greek city states
items.classification_rationale: Analytical capability requiring comparison across multiple frameworks - occasion-triggered skill

items.content_statement: Analyse similarities and differences in the role played by religion in society between the Elizabethan and Victorian periods
items.classification_rationale: Analytical capability requiring comparison across multiple interpretive lenses - occasion-triggered skill

items.content_statement: Evaluate the sources we use to find out about Ancient Greece and why it is sometimes difficult to be certain about how people lived at the time
items.classification_rationale: Analytical capability requiring source evaluation through multiple disciplinary lenses - occasion-triggered skill

items.content_statement: Compare how the Ancient Greek civilisation was similar to and different from the Egyptian civilisation
items.classification_rationale: Analytical capability requiring comparison across multiple interpretive frameworks - occasion-triggered skill

items.content_statement: Analyse the historical significance of Ancient Greece in shaping the modern world
items.classification_rationale: Analytical capability requiring evaluation of historical significance through multiple lenses - occasion-triggered skill

items.content_statement: Consider the similarities and differences in the monarch's power versus the power of Parliament and the people between the Elizabethan and Victorian periods
items.classification_rationale: Analytical capability requiring comparison across multiple dimensions - political power structures can be examined through different lenses with multiple valid interpretive frameworks

items.content_statement: Consider how life varied for different groups in Elizabethan and Victorian society
items.classification_rationale: Analytical capability requiring examination of social structures across periods - can be approached through multiple disciplinary lenses with reasoning quality determining assessment outcome

items.content_statement: Consider the different types of trade and extent of exploration between the Elizabethan and Victorian periods
items.classification_rationale: Analytical capability requiring comparative analysis of economic and exploratory activities - multiple valid interpretive frameworks exist for examining trade and exploration patterns

items.content_statement: Rome and its Empire: extent over time, including in Britain
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about historical facts - the geographical extent of Rome's Empire across different time periods. This is factual content that can be assessed through knowledge checks.

items.content_statement: Structure of Roman society
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about how Roman society was organized - factual content that learners must hold

items.content_statement: What is meant by a republic
items.classification_rationale: Definitional/conceptual knowledge - propositional content about the meaning of a republic

items.content_statement: How power was held and exercised in the Roman Republic

items.content_statement: Roman emperors and how they ruled the Roman Empire
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about historical facts and systems - who the emperors were and their methods of governance

items.content_statement: Why Rome stopped being a republic

items.content_statement: How rule by an emperor was different from rule by Senate in the Republic
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about historical governmental structures and their differences - conceptual understanding that can be assessed through explanation of the contrasts

items.content_statement: Roman gods and goddesses
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about specific deities - factual content that can be learned and assessed through knowledge checks

items.content_statement: Roman roads, baths, aqueducts, temples, forums
items.classification_rationale: Factual content about Roman infrastructure - propositional knowledge that can be learned and assessed through knowledge checks

items.content_statement: The role of slaves in Roman society
items.classification_rationale: This describes propositional knowledge about a historical topic that learners must understand - factual and conceptual content about how slavery functioned in Roman civilization

items.content_statement: the differences between monarchical rule (e.g. Egyptian Pharaohs or Roman kings) and the Roman Republic
items.classification_rationale: analytical capability across multiple lenses - comparing different governmental systems requires reasoning quality assessment

items.content_statement: the reasons why the Roman Republic ended

items.content_statement: the similarities and differences between Roman religion and beliefs in Ancient Egypt and Ancient Greece
items.classification_rationale: analytical capability across multiple lenses - comparative analysis of religious systems
